summary: Brunner's glands in the submucosa are the distinguishing feature of the duodenum.
Brunner's glands are found in the submucosa of the ileum.
Brunner's glands are located in the mucosa of the jejunum.
Peyer's patches (aggregated lymphoid follicles) are the distinguishing histological feature of the ileum.
Peyer's patches are the distinguishing feature of the duodenum.
Peyer's patches are found only in the colon.
The double-bubble sign on neonatal X-ray suggests duodenal atresia, caused by failure of recanalisation.
The double-bubble sign suggests pyloric stenosis caused by hypertrophy of pyloric muscle.
The double-bubble sign suggests ileal atresia caused by vascular compromise.
The GIT epithelium is derived from endoderm, while connective tissue and muscle are from splanchnic mesoderm.
The GIT epithelium is derived from ectoderm, while muscle is from endoderm.
Both GIT epithelium and muscle are derived from mesoderm.
Hirschsprung's disease results from failure of neural crest cell migration to the distal bowel, causing aganglionosis.
Hirschsprung's disease results from excessive neural crest cell migration causing hyperganglionosis.
Hirschsprung's disease is caused by failure of mesoderm differentiation in the gut wall.
Parietal cells in fundic glands secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor essential for vitamin B12 absorption.
Chief cells secrete hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor.
Parietal cells secrete pepsinogen and mucus.
The kidney develops from intermediate mesoderm in three waves: pronephros, mesonephros, and metanephros.
The kidney develops from lateral plate mesoderm in two waves: pronephros and metanephros.
The kidney develops from paraxial mesoderm in a single wave.
In the absence of testosterone, the Mullerian (paramesonephric) duct persists to form the female reproductive tract.
In the absence of testosterone, the Wolffian (mesonephric) duct persists to form the female reproductive tract.
The Mullerian duct requires testosterone to develop into the female reproductive tract.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review histology and embryology of the abdomen and pelvis.
